edges.evidence: StudentLife uses smartphone-based behavioral sensing to study student outcomes, directly continuing the mobile sensing paradigm introduced by Reality Mining.
The 2024 paper studies the same recursive-training failure mode as The Curse of Recursion but expands it into a broader and more mature analysis of model collapse.
RLAIF vs. RLHF explicitly investigates scaling alignment with AI feedback, a setup introduced in Constitutional AI.
Principle-Driven Self-Alignment uses principle-based self-critique and revision with minimal human supervision, closely following the constitutional alignment idea introduced by Constitutional AI.
Zephyr relies on direct preference optimization as a central alignment mechanism, building on the DPO formulation introduced earlier in 2023.
WizardLM extends Self-Instruct by automatically evolving seed instructions into more complex ones rather than only generating plain self-instruction data.
The self-concept degradation framework builds on concerns about models mirroring user beliefs and opinions, which are empirically examined in Whose Opinions Do Language Models Reflect?
This 2024 synthetic-content detection review discusses watermarking as a major detection strategy, directly building on A Watermark for Large Language Models.
The review covers robustness limits of AI-text detection and retrieval-based defenses, drawing directly on the findings of Paraphrasing Evades Detectors of AI-Generated Text.

LIMA argues that strong alignment can emerge from a very small, high-quality instruction set, challenging Self-Instruct's emphasis on large-scale synthetic instruction generation as the key ingredient.

notes: Additional edges are concentrated in three clusters: AI alignment papers around Constitutional AI, instruction-tuning papers around Self-Instruct, and synthetic-content detection papers around watermarking and detector robustness; a smaller sensing cluster links StudentLife to Reality Mining, and the 2024 model-collapse paper appears to extend the 2023 recursion-forgetting study.
